title: Air India's Maharaja Club: Reading a Loyalty Reboot
dek: The Flying Returns successor folds in Vistara members and moves to a points currency. How Maharaja Club is structured, and how to think about earning and redeeming value.
body:
Air India's loyalty programme has been rebuilt around a single brand, Maharaja Club, as the Tata-owned carrier integrates the former Vistara membership base into one ecosystem. The shift retired the long-running Flying Returns name, introduced a points-based currency and aligned tiers across a far larger combined airline. For frequent flyers in South Asia, it is one of the more consequential loyalty changes in years.

How the programme is structured

Maharaja Club uses a tiered model, with status levels rising from entry through to its top grades, and rewards accrued as Maharaja Points. Members earn on Air India flights, on Star Alliance partner carriers, and through non-airline partners, then redeem for award flights and cabin upgrades. As a Star Alliance member, Air India also lets status travellers tap a global network of partner benefits and lounges.

The headline change for many is currency and consolidation: bringing Vistara's loyal base under one roof avoids splitting a premium customer segment between two programmes, while a points structure gives Air India more flexibility to price awards dynamically.

How to think about value

Loyalty value is never just the points balance; it is what those points buy and how easily. A few principles travel well across any major programme, Maharaja Club included.

  • Premium-cabin redemptions and upgrades typically deliver the strongest value per point.
  • Alliance partners widen where points can be spent, but award availability and surcharges vary widely.
  • Status benefits, priority, baggage and lounge access, often matter more to regular flyers than the headline earn rate.

For now, the open question is execution: how generous award availability proves, how transparently points are valued, and how smoothly former Vistara members feel the transition. Those answers, more than the rebrand itself, will determine whether the reboot earns lasting loyalty.
